judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with accurate, well-grounded findings and concrete fixes; they are comparable.

report_1.evidence: `call_with_fallback` catches all of them identically via the single bare `except Exception as e:` — it logs a warning (`log_fallover`) and unconditionally resends the same `payload` to `secondary.complete(payload)`. There is no differentiation by error type at all.... **Fix:** replace the catch-all with an explicit non-retryable denylist that re-raises instead of failing over:

report_2.evidence: `BadRequestError` — indicates the request itself is malformed. Retrying the *identical* payload against a different vendor will very likely fail the same way... Inappropriate to fall over on this class.... Fix: catch the specific transient classes (`RateLimitError`, `RequestTimeoutError`, `ServerError`) and fall over to secondary only for those; handle `BadRequestError` and `ContentPolicyError` as their own non-retried cases (surface/quarantine rather than resend); treat `AuthError` as fail-fast

report_1.evidence: **Path A — non-production stage:** if `os.environ.get("STAGE")` is anything other than the literal string `"production"`... returns `True` unconditionally... **Path C — production, redis raises `RedisError`:** returns `True`, i.e. fails open.... invert both fail-open branches to fail closed, replace the env-name check with a narrow explicit dev-only override

report_2.evidence: **Path A** — `os.environ.get("STAGE") != "production"` → returns `True` unconditionally... silently disables rate limiting entirely rather than failing safe.... **Path C** ... → returns `True` (fail-open)... failing open during an infrastructure outage is the wrong default.... Fix: use an explicit allowlist/inversion for non-prod detection instead of `!= "production"`... and fail closed (return `False`... ) on `RedisError`

report_1.evidence: `record.input_text` — the full intake document text, i.e. the sensitive personal information the task states these documents contain, embedded whole in the log line. `record.metadata` — an arbitrary, unbounded dict passed straight through with no filtering.... **Fix:** log only an explicit allowlist, use the already-computed pseudonymized id, and hash/truncate free text

report_2.evidence: This writes the **raw, non-pseudonymized** `applicant_id` and the **full raw `input_text`** ... plus an arbitrary, unvalidated `metadata` dict... Fix: never log `input_text` (or replace with a redacted excerpt/length/hash if traceability is needed)... and whitelist specific non-sensitive fields from `metadata` rather than logging it wholesale.

report_1.evidence: if `PII_SALT` is unset ... the `.get(..., "")` default silently substitutes an empty string. There is no exception, no warning, no log line — the function degrades silently to `sha256(applicant_id)`, i.e. an **unsalted** hash of the raw applicant ID.... **Fix:** fail hard on a missing salt rather than defaulting

report_2.evidence: When `PII_SALT` is unset, `os.environ.get("PII_SALT", "")` silently defaults to the empty string — the function does not raise, warn, or log anything; it just returns `SHA256(applicant_id)`.... Fix: fail fast if `PII_SALT` is missing (`os.environ["PII_SALT"]`, raising `KeyError`, or an explicit startup-time config check) rather than silently defaulting to `""`
